Caption: Experimental versus predicted thermal conductivity for various salts using the KTM (Eq. 3) and PGM (Eq. 5). KTM predictions forego calculating the complexation factor ( ζ = 1 ), which would decrease predicted values for complexing salts. The same input parameters were used in both models, including thermodynamic mixing rules for mixture sound velocity and specific heat when experimental data were unavailable, as described in Sections 3.2 and 3.3 [55]. Comparisons are made at the minimum measured temperature of the available experimental data for LiF [56–58], NaF [57,59], KF [57], LiCl [60], NaCl, KCl [32,60], MgCl 2 [61], LiF-NaF [62,63], LiF-BeF 2 [64–66], LiF-NaF-KF [13,55,67,68], NaF-KF-MgF 2 [69], NaCl-CaCl 2 [70], NaCl-MgCl 2 -CaCl 2 [71], NaCl-UCl 3 [72,73], LiF-BeF 2 -UF 4 [66], LiF-BeF 2 -UF 4 -ZrF 4 , LiF-BeF 2 -ThF 4 -UF 4 [74], NaF-KF-UF 4 [75]. Literature sources are summarized in Table A.1.

Caption: The unweighted PDF of NaCl at 1074.15K, with a power law function fitted to the peaks of the Li–F g ( r ) of the form g ( r ) = a r − b + C , where C is the nominal probability of the ion pair to which g ( r ) decays (equal to 1 for the unweighted PDF). The potential of mean force ( W ( r ) ) is also shown for the Na–Cl pair, corresponding to the average interaction force. PDF is reproduced from Walz and Spoel [86].

Dispersion of molten NaCl at 1170 K with the adiabatic sound velocity and high frequency sound velocity [108]. Note the plateau in dispersion at λ = 2 a lattice , corresponding to twice the peak Na–Cl separation distance ( r = 2.67 Å) from the PDF of NaCl [86].

Parity plot of current structural coherence model (SCM) and KTM predictions compared to measurement data for dissociating, complexing, and actinide-bearing salts. Inset (b) provides the magnified section included in (a). All comparisons were made at the minimum measured temperature above the melting point.
